Facts.text: Old St. Peter's Basilica consecrated in Rome (stood 4th - 16th century), later replaced by current Basilica
Japanese emperor Kammu deallocates residence of Nara to Kioto
Maginulf is elected Antipope Sylvester IV
Pope Innocent III excommunicates Holy Roman Emperor Otto IV
Pope Boniface VIII issues papal bull (decree) "Unam sanctam" emphasizing the higher position of the spiritual in comparison with the secular order
William Tell reputedly shoots apple off his son's head
Southern sea floods 72 villages, killing estimated 10,000 in the Netherlands
Storm flood ravages Dutch coast
Seventh Council of Toledo of bishops convened by Visigothic King of Hispania Chindasuinth, extends treason laws to the clergy
Treaty of Bongaja: King Hassan-Udin of Makasar & VOC
Charles François Felix operates on King Louis XIV of France's anal fistula after practising the surgery on several peasants.
France & Austria sign peace treaty
Prussia and Great Britain sign an anti-French military covenant
Worst quake in Massachusetts Bay area strikes Boston; no deaths reported
First Unitarian minister in US ordained, Boston
Battle of Vertieres; Haitian population inflict final defeat on French force attempting to quell slave rebellion on colony of Saint-Dominque
Pulver Purim is first celebrated to commemorate miraculous escape of Rabbi Abraham Danzig from a gunpowder explosion in Vilna
Antarctica sighted by US Navy Captain Nathaniel B. Palmer
Netherlands & Belgium sign Treaty of Zonhoven
Rose Philippine Duchesne dies in St. Charles, Missouri. She would be canonized on July 3, 1988 by Pope John Paul II.
The first provisional meeting of the Confederate Congress is held in Richmond, Virginia
National Woman's Christian Temperance Union organizes in Cleveland, Ohio
US and Canadian railroads set and synchronize four standard time zones - Eastern, Central, Mountain, and Pacific, replacing over 100 previous time zones
Oahu Railway begins public service in Hawaii
Richard Outcault's early comic strip "Origin of a New Species" published in World
Trumper scores 208 in 185 mins (1 five 25 fours) NSW v Qld
The USA and Great Britain sign the Second Hay-Pauncefote Treaty, or Interoceanic Ship Canal Treaty
Brooklyn toymaker Morris Michtom names the teddy bear after US President Teddy Roosevelt
Hay-Bunau-Varilla Treaty gives US exclusive canal rights in Panama
General Esteban Huertas steps down after the government of Panama fears he wants to stage a coup
Prince Carl of Denmark becomes King Haakon VII of Norway
Anarchists bomb St. Peter's Basilica in Rome
Langdon Mitchell's "New York Idea" premieres in NYC
US invades Nicaragua, later overthrows President Zelaya
Britain's first seaplane flies
First American performance of Ludwig Thuille's opera "Lobetanz", Metropolitan Opera in New York City
Cholera breaks out in Constantinople, in the Ottoman Empire
Lincoln Beachey becomes the first American pilot to perform an aircraft loop-the-loop in his Curtiss aeroplane near San Diego
Sigma Alpha Rho, a Jewish high school fraternity, is founded in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.
Belgian troops re-enter Brussels, lost to the German invaders on 20 August 1914
Latvia declares independence from Russia
H. Tierney & J. McCarthy's musical "Irene" premieres in NYC
Apollo Theater (Academy, Bryant) opens at 221 W 42nd St NYC
Turkish National Assembly nominates Abdülmecid II as caliph - the last Ottoman caliph

George Bernard Shawaccepts the Nobel Prize for Literature but refuses the prize money, saying "I can forgive Alfred Nobel for inventing dynamite, but only a fiend in human form could have invented the Nobel Prize"
Foundation stone for Umaid Bhawan Palace laid in Jodhpur, India. Originally built to provide employment for drought-stricken farmers, now one of world's largest private residences
Large earthquake in Atlantic breaks transatlantic cable in 28 places
Sōka Kyōiku Gakkai, a Buddhist association later renamed Soka Gakkai, is founded by Japanese educators Tsunesaburo Makiguchi and Josei Toda.
"Flowers & Trees" receives 1st Academy Award for a cartoon
Main span of Golden Gate Bridge joined
Trade union members elect John L. Lewis as the first president of the Congress of Industrial Organizations.
Dutch KNSM passenger ship Simón Bolívar hits German mine, 86 die
The Irish Republican Army explodes three bombs in Piccadilly Circus
George Matesky, New York City's Mad Bomber places his first bomb at a Manhattan office building used by Consolidated Edison.
British troops open attack on Tobruk, North Africa
Jerome Chodorov/Joseph Fields' "Junior Miss" premieres in NYC
444 British bombers attack Berlin
First US ambassador to Canada, Ray Atherton, nominated
WWII: German submarine U-211 sunk east of the Azores in the Atlantic Ocean by British aircraft
The U.S. Air Force grounds B-29s after two crashes and 23 deaths in three days
"See it Now" premieres on TV
British troops occupy Ismailiya, Egypt
Former Cubs 1st baseman & future TV star of Rifleman Chuck Connors is 1st player to oppose the major league draft
Anti-Revolutionary Party (ARP) accept female suffrage
Bell X-2 rocket plane taken up for 1st powered flight
Tunisia refuses Russian weapons
1st true reservoir in Jerusalem opens
Indians minority stockholders sell their stock to William Delay
Wash Senator Bob Allison wins AL Rookie of Year
Charlie Finley makes a bid to purchase expansion LA Angels
Copyright office issues its 10 millionth registration
US Ranger 2 launched to Moon; failed
Bell Telephone introduces the touch-tone telephone to customers in Pennsylvania
Dartford-Purfleet tunnel under River Thames opens
Twins SS Zoilo Versalles is named AL MVP
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
US Roman Catholic bishops ends rules against eating meat on Fridays
British government devalues pound from US equivalent of $2.80 to $2.40
Soviets recover the Zond 6 spacecraft after a flight around the moon
Prosecutor Vincent Bugliosi assigned to the Tate and LaBianca murders, will eventually convict Charles Mason for orchestrating the killings
Netherlands & Albania form diplomatic relations
Russia lands self propelled rover on Moon
A British soldier is shot dead by the Irish Republican Army (IRA) in Belfast
China performs nuclear test at Lop Nor, PRC
Arab oil ministers cancel the scheduled 5 percent cut in production for EEC
Greek regime calls emergency crisis due to mass protests
Black Panther leader Eldridge Cleaver returns to US
Calvin Murphy (Houston) ends NBA free throw streak of 58 games
MLB New York Yankees sign free agent pitcher Don Gullett
Spain's parliament establishes democracy after 37 yrs of dictatorship
Great Britain performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Leo J Ryan, American politician, and 4 others, killed in Jonestown, Guyana by members of Peoples Temple, followed by ritual mass suicide of 914 members of the religious cult
Argentina announces its ability to produce enriched uranium for nuclear weapons
MGM/UA releases nostalgic holiday film "A Christmas Story", starring Peter Billingsley and Darren McGavin, based on anecdotes from humorist Jean Shepherd
Browns set team records for most sacks (11)
Flyers' Ron Sutter fails on 11th penalty shot against Islanders
NBC premiere of the first part of fact based crime mystery "Fatal Vision", based on Joe McGinnis' novel about Jefferey MacDonald and the 1970 murders of his then-pregnant wife and two children
NJ Devils shutout NY Rangers 6-0
The Soviet Union helps deliver American wheat during the Ethiopian famine
Enterprise (OV-101) flies from Kennedy Space Center to Dulles Airport
Howard Stern Radio Show returns to NYC (WXRK 92.3 FM) with an afternoon drive slot
31 people die in a fire at King's Cross, London's busiest tube station
Congressional committee reports on Iran-Contra affair
Pennsylvania is first to restrict abortions after US Supreme Court gave states the right to do so
NFL NY Giants beat Det Lions 20-0, to run 1990 record to 10-0
Auburn men's basketball team placed on 2 yr probation for recruiting violations and not eligible for post-season play 1991-92
France deports Marlon's daughter Cheyenne Brando to Tahiti
Muslim Shi'ites release hostages Terry Waite & Thomas Sutherland
Howard Stern Radio Show premieres in Las Vegas, Nevada on KXTE 107.5 FM
27 killed at prison in Morazan, El Salvador
Black and white leaders in South Africa approve a new democratic constitution
North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) passes US House of Representatives
North-Siberia record cold for November (-55°C)
"Star Trek: First Contact" film directed by Jonathan Frakes and starringPatrick Stewartand Jonathan Frakes premieres
"Titanic: Music from the Motion Picture" album is released by Sony Music Soundtrax
Arizona Diamondbacks & Tampa Bay Devil Rays expansion draft
FBI says no evidence of foul play in 1996 TWA 800 crash
Mavericks' A. C. Green ties Randy Smith's NBA record of 906 cons games
Rare black pearl necklace auctioned for record $902,000
Warner Bros. releases "Songs from The Capeman", ninth solo studio album by American singer-songwriterPaul Simon; it contains Simon's own performances of songs from his Broadway musical flop
Willem de Kooning painting "Two Standing Women" sold for $4,182,500
In College Station, Texas, 12 are killed and 27 injured at Texas A&M University when a massive bonfire under construction collapses.
Home crowd favourite Leyton Hewitt wins his first of 2 season-ending Tennis Masters Cup titles with a 6–3, 6–3, 6–4 victory over Frenchman Sébastien Grosjean in Sydney, Australia
Phillips Petroleum and Conoco merge into a new company as 'ConocoPhillips', the third-largest oil and natural gas company in the US
Iraq disarmament crisis: United Nations weapons inspectors led by Hans Blix arrive in Iraq
In England, the Local Government Act 2003, repealing controversial anti-gay amendment Section 28, becomes effective
Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court rules the state's ban on same-sex marriages is unconstitutional
The congress of the Communist Party of Indian Union (Marxist-Leninist) decides to merge the party into Kanu Sanyal's CPI(ML).
Russia officially ratifies the Kyoto Protocol
Video game Minecraft is officially released by Mojang
Israeli Gaza rocket strikes kill 80 alleged terrorist targets
20 people are killed after a train collides with a minibus in Cairo, Egypt
Jamaica win the 2014 Caribbean Cup in football
2 female suicide bombers aged 18 and 11 blow themselves up in Kano, Nigeria, killing 15 and injuring over 100
French police raid terrorist cell in Saint Denis, killing 2 including the leader of the Paris terror attacks Abdelhamid Abaaoud
American missionary John Allen Chau killed on forbidden North Sentinel Island, Bay of Bengal by one of world's most isolated tribe
APEC Summit in Papa New Guinea fails to produce a joint agreement for first time in two decades after US and China clash on definition of trade
Deforestation of Brazil's Amazon rainforest worst since 2008, has lost 9,762 sq km (3,769 sq miles) of vegetation in 12 months according to country's Space Agency
World wind speeds have risen, 3x faster since 2010 than previous decades of decline, according to Princeton study published in "Nature Climate Change"
Floods and landslides effect more than 3 million people, killing at least 70 in wake of Typhoon Vamco in Cagayan Province, Philippines
Thailand's parliament agrees to reforms, but not to the monarchy, after massive public protests were met by tear gas and water canons
US COVID-19 death toll passes 250,000, recorded cases at 11.5 million, hospitalizations at 76,830 amid a country-wide surge
US judge exonerates Muhammad Abdul Aziz and Khalil Islam for the killing ofMalcolm Xin 1965, saying they were "wrongly convicted", after 55 years
International Bureau of Weights and Measures votes to abandon the leap second (to take effect 2035). Originally inserted in 1972 to reconcile atomic and astronomical time scales
Russian vetoes a UK-backed ceasefire plan to end Sudan's 19-month civil war at the UN Security Council
First English dated printed book "Dictes & Sayengis of the Phylosophers" byWilliam Caxtonis printed at his press in London
French KingCharles VIIIoccupies Florence
Portuguese navigatorVasco da Gamareaches the Cape of Good Hope
Voltaire's first play, the tragedy "Oedipe" premieres in Paris, and first use of non de plume Voltaire by François-Marie Arouet
Bonnie Prince Charlie's Jacobite army occupy Carlisle during its invasion of England
Letter by Göttingen Seven published protesting abolition of constitution of Kingdom of Hanover, by seven professors including Jacob and WilhelmGrimm
State funeral ofDuke of Wellingtonis held at St Paul's Cathedral in London
Mark Twainpublishes the short story "The Celebrated Jumping Frog of Calaveras County" in The New York Saturday Press
SuffragetteSusan B. Anthonyis arrested by a U.S. Deputy Marshal and charged with illegally voting
Antonín Dvořák's "Husitska" (Hussite Overture) premieres at the gala opening of the Prague National Theater
PopeLeo XIIIpublishes encyclical Providentissimus Deus
George Bernard Shaw's play "Major Barbara" premieres in London
PopePius XIencyclical On persecution of Church in Mexico
Walt Disney's "Steamboat Willie" is released, the first Mickey Mouse sound cartoon
Musical "Smiles" withBob HopeandFred Astairepremieres in NYC
5th Academy Awards:Fredric MarchandWallace Beeryin 1st ever tie for Best Actor. "Grand Hotel" andHelen Hayesalso win.
Germany & Italy recognize Spanish government ofFrancisco Franco
Benito Mussolini's Italian forces leave Abyssinia/Ethiopia, forced out by Allied attacks
Thornton Wilder's play "Skin of our Teeth" premieres in NYC (Pulitzer Prize for Drama 1943)
Arnold Schoenberg's Prelude for orchestra & mixed choir, premieres
NL batting leader (.342)Jackie Robinsonwins NL MVP
South Korean PresidentSyngman Rheeforced to end mass executions
Soviet leaderNikita Khrushchevsays the phrase "we will bury you!" to Western ambassadors at a reception at the Polish embassy in Moscow
"Ben-Hur" directed byWilliam Wylerand starringCharlton Hestonpremieres in New York City (Academy Awards Best Picture 1960)
JFKsends 18,000 military advisors to South Vietnam
KingHassan IIopens 1st parliament in Morocco
Baltimore Orioles third basemanBrooks Robinsonwins American League MVP
J. Edgar HooverdescribesMartin Luther King Jr.as a "most notorious liar"
Brooklyn/Los Angeles Dodgers' pitcherSandy Koufaxannounces his retirement due to an arthritic left elbow
American scientistLinus Paulingdeclares large doses of Vitamin C could ward off colds (never proven definitively)
Future HOF boxerJoe FrazierKOs defending champion Bob Foster in 2nd round at Cobo Arena, Detroit, Michigan for the WBA, WBC and The Ring heavyweight titles
Johnny Benchwins NL MVP
"The Lamb Lies Down on Broadway" album by English progressive rock bandGenesisis released, their last to feature original frontmanPeter Gabriel
"Rufus featuringChaka Khan" 4th studio album by Rufus is released (Billboard Album of the Year 1976)
In Jonestown, Guyana, 918 members of the Peoples Temple are murdered or commit suicide under the leadership of cult leaderJim Jones
Ayatollah Khomeinicharges US ambassador and embassy with espionage
"Heaven's Gate" film directed by Michael Cimino, starringKris KristoffersonandChristopher Walkenpremieres - one of the biggest box office bombs ever
Despite missing 45 games,George Brettwins AL MVP
Phillies 3rd basemanMike Schmidtwins his 2nd consecutive NL MVP
South Korean boxer Duk Koo Kim dies from injuries sustained during a 14-round beating byRay Manciniin Las Vegas; WBC shortens title bouts to 12 rounds; WBA and WBO follow in 1988, and IBF in 1989
Dwight Gooden(NL) and Bret Saberhagen (AL) win Cy Young Award
Paul McCartneyreleases film theme single "Spies Like Us"
Roger Clemenswins AL MVP
CubsAndre Dawsonis 1st player from a last-place club to win an MVP
1st Solheim Cup Women's Golf, Lake Nona G & CC: US beats Europe 11½-4½ in the inaugural event;Kathy Whitworthand Mickey Walker are the US and European captains
Andre Agassiclaims his first and only season-ending ATP Tour World Championship tennis title with a 5–7, 7–6, 7–5, 6–2 win over defending championStefan Edbergin Frankfurt, Germany
Yugoslavian tennis starMonica SelesbeatsGabriela Sabatiniof Argentina 6–4, 5–7, 3–6, 6–4, 6–2 to win her first of 3 straight WTA Tour Championships in NYC; first 5-set women's final since 1901
The Croatian city of Vukovar surrenders to Yugoslav People's Army and allied Serb paramilitary forces after an 87-day siege
"Malcolm X" film directed bySpike LeestarringDenzel Washingtonand Angela Bassett is released in the US
American rock bandNirvanafilms a mostly acoustic set at Sony Music Studios in New York City, for the television series MTV Unplugged
Pearl Jamlead singerEddie Vedderarrested for public drunkiness
"Star Trek: Generations" film directed by David Carson and starringPatrick Stewartpremieres
Eappen family hireLouise Woodwardas their nanny, later charged with murder and convicted of involuntary manslaughter
70s glam-rock starGary Glitter(real name Paul Gadd) arrested by British police in child porn probe
Metallicarelease the album "ReLoad"
Dark Horse/EMI posthumously releases "Brainwashed",George Harrison's 12th and final studio album
Mercury Nashville Records releases "Up!", the 4th studio album byShania Twain(Billboard Album of the Year, 2003)
20th Century Fox releases "Walk the Line", starringJoaquin PhoenixasJohnny CashandReese WitherspoonasJune Carter, and directed by James Mangold; Witherspoon wins an Academy Award
Roger Federerscores back-to-back season-ending Tennis Masters Cup titles beating Spaniard David Ferrer 6–2, 6–3, 6–2 in the final in Shanghai, China
"The X Factor" groupOne Directionrelease their debut album "Up All Night" in Ireland and the UK
Former Filipino PresidentGloria Macapagal-Arroyois arrested and held at Veterans Memorial Medical Center in Quezon City under charges of electoral sabotage
Lewis Hamiltonwins the 2012 US Formula One Grand Prix
American singer-songwriterWillie Nelsonreceives Library of Congress Gershwin Prize at the Thomas Jefferson Building in Washington, D.C.
Motoring TV series "The Grand Tour" premieres on Amazon Prime Video, starringJeremy Clarkson,Richard HammondandJames May
Shawn Mendesis the first singer under 18 to get three No. 1 singles on Billboard's Adult Contemporary chart
21-year-old GermanAlexander Zverevbreaks through for a maiden season-ending ATP World Tour Finals tennis title with a 6-4, 6-3 upset win in London over 5-time championNovak Đoković
Book written by Charlotte Bronte aged 14 for her toy soldiers bought by the Bronte Society for €600,000 at auction in Paris
Secretary of StateMike Pompeoreverses US policy regarding Israeli West Bank settlements as illegal after 24 years
Michael B. Jordannamed "Sexiest Man Alive" by People Magazine
St. Peter's Basilica is consecrated in Rome, replacing an earlier basilica on the same site and becoming the world's largest Christian church
The original Westminster Bridge across the River Thames in London is opened in a midnight ceremony
British GeneralDouglas Haigfinally calls off the First Battle of the Somme in World War I after more than 1 million soldiers are killed or wounded
"Kangaroo Dundee" wildlife TV series premieres featuring Brolga and Roger the ripped Kangaroo on BBC Two
Kōnin, 49th Emperor of Japan (770-81) and last emperor of the Nara period, born in Japan (d. 782) [birthdate disputed]
Lamoral, Count of Egmont, Prince of Gavere, Netherlandic noble, general, and statesman, born in La Hamaide, Ellezelles, Spanish Netherlands (d. 1568)
Luca Cambiaso, Italian painter and sculptor, born in Moneglia, Republic of Genoa (d. 1585)
Gaspar de Crayer, Flemish painter, born in Antwerp (d. 1669)
Eleonora Gonzaga, the Younger, Holy Roman Empress (1651-57) by marriage to Emperor Ferdinand III, born in Mantua, Duchy of Mantua (d. 1686)
Jean-Baptiste Loeillet, Flemish composer, born in Ghent, Spanish Netherlands (d. 1730)
Philibert Commerçon, French naturalist, born in Châtillon-les-Dombes, France (d. 1773)
Carl Friedrich Christian Fasch, German composer, born in Zerbst, Anhalt-Zerbst, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1800)
Félix Máximo López, Spanish composer, born in Madrid, Spain (d. 1821)
Louis Ferdinand, Prussian prince and a soldier in the Napoleonic Wars, born in Friedrichsfelde Palace, Berlin (d. 1806)
Tokugawa Ienari, 11th and longest serving Japanese shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate (1787-1837), born in Edo, Japan (d. 1841)
Wilhelmine of Prussia, Queen of the Netherlands (1815-37), born in Potsdam, Prussia (d. 1837)
Felice Blangini, Italian composer, born in Turin, Kingdom of Sardinia (d. 1841)
David Wilkie, British painter, born in Pitlessie Fife, Scotland (d. 1841)
Henry Bishop, British composer and conductor, born in London (d. 1855)
Cornelis Broere, Dutch Roman Catholic priest and poet, born in Amsterdam (d. 1860)
Alfonso Ferrero la Marmora, Italian general and statesman (6th Prime Minister of Italy, 1864-66), born in Turin, French Empire (d. 1878)
Antoine-Amable-Elie Elwart, French composer, born in Paris (d. 1877)
Asa Gray, American botanist (Flora of North America), born in Sauquoit, New York (d. 1888)
Benjamin Stone Roberts, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Manchester, Vermont (d. 1875)
Jesse J. Finley, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Lebanon, Tennessee (d. 1904)
James William Abert, American army officer and explorer who travelled the Canadian River, born in Mount Holly, New Jersey (d. 1897)
Franz Sigel, German-American teacher, newspaperman, politician, and Major General (Union Army), born in Sinsheim, Grand Duchy of Baden (d. 1902)
Isham Nicholas Haynie, American lawyer, politician, and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Dover, Tennessee (d. 1868)
Adolf Erik Nordenskiöld, Finnish-Swedish explorer and mineralogist (Vega Expedition), born in Helsinki, Finland (d. 1901)
Americus Vespucius Rice, American politician, banker, and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Perrysville, Ohio (d. 1904)
August Kundt, German physicist (sound vibration, test of Kundt), born in Schwerin, Mecklenburg, Germany (d. 1894)
Jacob-Ferdinand Mellaerts, Belgian Catholic priest, social worker and founder of the first Christian Farmers' Guild, born in Binkom, Belgium (d. 1925)[1]
Prince Louis of Liechtenstein, Liechtenstein nobleman and politician, born in Prague (d. 1920)

Nikolaĭ Nikolaevich, Russian noble (Grand Duke of Russia; disputed Emperor, 1922-23), and military officer (Imperial Army Commander, 1914-18), born in St. Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1929) [NS date]
Dorothy Dix [Elizabeth Meriwether Gilmer], American journalist and columnist, born in Woodstock, Tennessee (d. 1951)
Richard Dehmel, German poet and writer, born in Hermsdorf, Brandenburg, Prussia (d. 1920)
Amadeo Vives, Spanish composer, born in Collbató, Spain (d. 1932)
Margaret Seddon, American stage and screen actress (Mr. Deeds Goes to Town), born in Washington, DC (d. 1968)
Clarence Day, American author (Life With Father), born in New York City (d. 1935)
Riccardo Martin, American tenor and composer, born in Hopkinsville, Kentucky (d. 1952)
Arthur Cecil Pigou, English economist (Economics of Welfare), born in Ryde, Isle of Wight, England (d. 1959)
Percy LeSueur, Canadian hockey goaltender (inventor large goalie glove & LeSueur net), born in Quebec City, Quebec (d. 1962)
Amelita Galli-Curci, Italian-American operatic soprano (Cave of the Winds), born in Milan, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1963)
Jacques Maritain, French Catholic philosopher (exponent of St Thomas), born in Paris (d. 1973)
Wyndham Lewis, English author (Tarr, Apes of God) and co-founder of the Vorticist movement in art, born in Amherst, Nova Scotia (d. 1957)
Carl Vinson, American politician (38th Dean of the United States House of Representatives, 1961-65), born in Baldwin County, Georgia (d. 1981)
Frances Marion, American screenwriter and actress (Pollyanna), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1973)
Tirumalai Krishnamacharya, Indian yoga instructor and scholar known as "The Father of Modern Yoga", born in Southern India (d. 1989)
Ernst Levy, Swiss composer, born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 1981)
Jules Buffano, American composer and pianist (Jimmy Durante Show), born in St Louis, Missouri (d. 1960)
Patrick Blackett, British physicist (Nobel 1948 - nuclear reaction), born in London (d. 1974)
Erich Sehlbach, German composer, born in Barmen, Germany (d. 1985)
Joris Ivens, Dutch director (A Tale of the Wind, Rain), born in Nijmegen, Netherlands (d. 1989)
Howard Thurman, African-American theologian and author (Deep River, Deep is the Hunger), born in Daytona Beach, Florida (d. 1981)
Constantin Alajalov, Armenian-American illustrator (The New Yorker; Saturday Evening Post; Dithers & Jitters), and painter, born in Rostov-on-Don, Russia (d. 1987)
George Kistiakowsky, Ukrainian-American chemist who helped develop the first atomic bomb (Manhattan Project), but later opposed nuclear weapons, born in Kyiv, Ukraine (d. 1982)
Barbara Giuranna, Italian pianist and composer, born in Palermo, Italy (d. 1998)
Jorgen Nielsen, Danish author (regional novels), born in Pårup, Denmark (d. 1945)
Lillian Fuchs, American violist and composer, born in New York (d. 1995)
Guido Santórsola, Brazilian-Uruguayan composer and violinist, born in Canosa di Puglia, Italy (d. 1994)
Jean Paul Lemieux, Canadian painter, born in Quebec (d. 1990)
Klaus Mann, German-American writer (Mephisto; The Turning Point), born in Munich, Germany (d. 1949)
Compay Segundo [Máximo Muñoz-Telles], Cuban trova guitarist, singer, and composer (Buena Vista Social Club), born in Siboney, Cuba (d. 2003)
Halldis Moren Vesaas, Norwegian author and poet (Harp and Dagger), born in Trysil, Norway (d. 1995)
Friedrich Weinreb, Polish economist and narrative author, born in Lemberg, Galicia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1988)
Attilio Bertolucci, Italian poet and writer, born in San Lazzaro Parmense, Italy (d. 2000)
Arthur Peterson, American actor (Soap, Mission: Impossible, Crisis), born in Mandan, North Dakota (d. 1996)
Jaap Meijer, Dutch historian and rabbi in Paramaribo, born in Winschoten, Netherlands (d. 1993)
Leif Solberg, Norwegian organist, choral conductor and composer, born in Lena, Norway (d. 2016)
Ken Burkhart, American baseball pitcher (St. Louis Cardinals, Cincinnati Reds) and MLB umpire (NL 1957-73), born in Knoxville, Tennessee (d. 2004)
James L. Lyons, American jazz promoter (Monterey Jazz Festival, 1958-92), born in China (d. 1994)
Tasker Watkins, Welsh World War II hero, born in Nelson, Wales (d. 2007)
Jocelyn Brando, American actress (The Big Heat, Ugly American, China Venture), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2005)
Bill Bedford, British test pilot (pioneered the development of V/STOL aircraft), born in Loughborough, England (d. 1996)
Louis Alfred Mennini, American composer, born in Erie, Pennsylvania (d. 2000)
Mustafa Khalil, 40th Prime Minister of Egypt (1978-80), born in Al Qalyubiyah Governorate, Egypt (d. 2008)
Al Dvorin, American Elvis Presley concert announcer, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2004)
Luis Somoza Debayle, President of Nicaragua (1956-63), born in León, Nicaragua (d. 1967)
Marjorie Gestring, American diver (Olympic gold 3m springboard 1936; International Swimming HOF), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1992)
Viktor Afanasyev, Soviet politician and editor (Pravda), born in Aktamysh, Tatar A.S.S.R., Russian Soviet Federated Socialist Republic (d. 1994)
Ted Stevens, American politician (U.S. Senator from Alaska, 1968-2009), born in Indianapolis, Indiana (d. 2010)
Alexander Mackenzie Stuart, Baron Mackenzie-Stuart, Scottish jurist, born in Aberdeen, Scotland (d. 2000)
Alex Macintosh, British BBC presenter and actor (Hell Fight), born in Fulham, London (d. 1997)
William Mayer, American composer (A Death in the Family; Brief Candle; Octagon), born in Manhattan, New York City (d. 2017)
Yvonne Bryceland, South African stage actress (Road to Mecca), born in Cape Town, South Africa (d. 1992)
Dorothy Collins [Marjorie Chandler], Canadian-American singer and actress (Your Hit Parade), born in Windsor, Ontario (d. 1994)
Estanislao Basora, Spanish footballer (The Monster of Colombes, 22 caps for Spain, 301 matches for Barcelona), born in Barcelona, Catalonia (d. 2012)
Kim Besly, British peace activist (Greenham Common), birthplace unknown (d. 1996)
Eldar Ryazanov, Russian film director and screenwriter (A Cruel Romance), born in Kuybyshev, USSR (d. 2015)
Hank Ballard [John Henry Kendricks], American rocker (The Midnighters - "The Twist" - pre-Chubber Checker), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 2003)
Lawrence Moss, American contemporary classical, opera, and electronic music composer, and educator, born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2022)
Otar Gordeli, Georgian composer, born in Tbilisi, Georgia (d. 1994)
Salvador Laurel, Filipino lawyer and politician (Vice President of the Philippines, 1986-92), born in Paco, Manila, Insular Government of the Philippine Islands (d. 2004)
Sheila Jordan (née Dawson), American jazz singer and songwriter, born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 2025)[1][2]
Francisco Savín, Mexican conductor and composer (Quasar I), born in Mexico City (d. 2018)
William J. "Pete" Knight, American astronaut and test pilot (X-15), born in Noblesville, Indiana (d. 2004)
Sonja Edström-Ruthström, Swedish cross-country skier (Olympic gold 3 × 5k relay 1960), born in Luleå, Sweden (d. 2020)
Vassilis Vassilikos, Greek writer (Z) and diplomat, born in Kavala, Greece
Pervis Staples, American gospel vocalist (The Staple Singers - "Uncloudy Day"), born in Drew, Mississippi (d. 2021)
Rodney Hall, Australian author and poet, born in Solihull, England
Rudolf Bahro, German dissident, politician and Marxist philosopher imprisoned by East Germany, born in Bad Flinsberg, Germany (d. 1997)
Brian Huggett, Welsh golfer (British Open 1965 runner-up; Harry Vardon Trophy 1968; 10 x European Senior Tour titles), born in Porthcawl, Wales (d. 2024)
Don Cherry, American jazz trumpeter (Ornette Coleman), born in Oklahoma City (d. 1995)
Karl Schranz, Austrian alpine skier (World C'ship gold Downhill, Combined 1962; Giant slalom 1970), born in St. Anton, Austria
Amanda Lear, French pop singer, born in Saigon, French Indochina [some sources give date as June 18]
